title: Advantages, Limitations, and Terminology
narrationScript: Every test method earns its place by what it does well, and keeps its place through inspectors who know exactly what it cannot do. This lesson covers both sides for magnetic particle testing, and then nails down the vocabulary we will use for the rest of the course. Start with the strengths. MT is fast. A yoke test on a weld takes minutes, and a bench unit can process hundreds of parts in a shift. It is relatively inexpensive. The consumables are powder and bath, not film, couplant, or elaborate probes. It gives you an indication directly on the part, right at the discontinuity, shaped roughly like the discontinuity, so interpretation is visual and immediate. It finds the killers, the tight surface cracks. Fatigue cracks, grinding cracks, quench cracks, seams and laps, the discontinuities that grow under load. And unlike penetrant testing, it can also pull indications from discontinuities slightly below the surface, it can work through a thin, tightly adherent coating, and it tolerates surfaces that would trap penetrant. Surface preparation is far less fussy than penetrant's. Light cleaning is usually enough. Portable yokes go up scaffolding and inside tanks. Those are real advantages, and they are why MT is everywhere that steel is welded, forged, and machined. Now the limits, and respect every one of them. First and absolute, the part must be ferromagnetic. Iron and most steels, yes. Aluminum, copper, titanium, magnesium, and the austenitic stainless steels, no. No magnetism, no leakage field, no test. Second, MT is a surface and near surface method only. A flaw half an inch deep is invisible to it. That is ultrasonic or radiographic territory. Third, direction matters. Particles gather where flux crosses a discontinuity, and detection is best when the flux runs between forty five and ninety degrees across it. A crack lying parallel to the flux barely disturbs it and may show nothing at all. That is why one magnetizing shot is never a complete test, and full coverage takes two magnetizations roughly perpendicular to each other. Fourth, the method puts current or flux through hardware, and that brings cautions. Arc burns from poor contact at heads or prods, possible damage on finished surfaces, and many aerospace specifications prohibit prod contact entirely. Fifth, thick coatings mask indications, demagnetization is often required after testing, and parts with complex shapes can be genuinely awkward to field properly. None of these limits is a defect of the method. They are its boundaries, and a professional states them plainly. Now the vocabulary, because precise words are half of accurate reporting. A discontinuity is any interruption in the normal structure of a part. A crack, a seam, a lap, an inclusion. A defect is a discontinuity that has been evaluated against the acceptance criteria and rejected. The word means failed, so never use it casually. An indication is what the test actually shows you, the particle pattern, and indications come in three kinds. A relevant indication results from an actual discontinuity. A nonrelevant indication results from a real magnetic effect that is not a discontinuity, such as a change in section, a drilled hole just under the surface, or the boundary between metals of different permeability in a weld. A false indication has no magnetic cause at all. Particles held by gravity, by drainage, or by surface roughness. Interpretation is deciding which kind of indication you have. Evaluation is judging a relevant indication against the acceptance criteria. Then come the physics terms you will use daily. Magnetic flux is the magnetic field passing through the part. Flux density, symbol B, is how concentrated that flux is, measured in gauss or tesla, and ten thousand gauss equals one tesla. Magnetizing force, symbol H, is the effort applied to create the flux, measured in oersteds. Permeability is how easily a material accepts flux, B divided by H, and it is what separates ferromagnetic materials, which concentrate flux strongly, from paramagnetic and diamagnetic materials, which barely respond. Retentivity is a material's ability to hold a residual field after the magnetizing force is removed, and hard, high carbon steels hold far more than soft, low carbon ones, which is why only high retentivity parts qualify for the residual method. Coercive force is the reverse magnetizing force it takes to wipe that residual field back to zero. Learn these words now, and use them exactly. Every lesson that follows speaks this language, and so does every examiner you will ever sit in front of.
imagePrompt: Under ultraviolet light in a darkened inspection booth, glowing green-yellow fluorescent crack indications bloom along the weld toe of a heavy steel fixture as a gloved technician leans in close to examine them.

sections.heading: What MT Does Well
sections.body:
Inside its lane, MT is one of the most efficient inspections in the toolbox:

- **Speed and cost.** A yoke examination of a weld takes minutes; a bench unit processes hundreds of parts per shift. Consumables are powder or bath - cheap next to film or probe systems.
- **Direct, intuitive indications.** The particle pattern forms **on the part, at the discontinuity**, in roughly its size, shape, and orientation. Interpretation is immediate and visual.
- **Sensitivity to the killers.** Tight surface cracks - fatigue, grinding, quench - plus seams and laps are exactly what MT renders most vividly.
- **Near-surface reach.** Unlike penetrant, MT can indicate discontinuities **slightly below the surface** (the indication grows broader and fuzzier with depth).
- **Tolerant of real-world surfaces.** Light cleaning usually suffices; thin, tight coatings are workable within procedure limits; surfaces that would trap penetrant are often still testable.
- **Portability.** Yokes and prod kits go up scaffolding, into tanks, and out to the field.

That combination - fast, cheap, sensitive, portable - is why MT is everywhere steel is welded, forged, machined, or overhauled.

sections.heading: The Hard Limits
sections.body:
Respect every one of these boundaries; exam questions and field failures both live here.

- **Ferromagnetic materials only.** Iron and most steels test beautifully. Aluminum, magnesium, copper, titanium, and **austenitic stainless steels** cannot be usefully magnetized: no flux, no leakage field, no test.
- **Surface and near-surface only.** Sensitivity falls off rapidly with depth. Internal flaws belong to UT and RT.
- **Direction sensitivity.** Particles collect where flux *crosses* a discontinuity - best at **45 to 90 degrees**. A flaw lying parallel to the flux may show nothing, so a single magnetization is never complete coverage: **two roughly perpendicular magnetizations** are required.
- **Electrical and surface damage risk.** Direct-contact techniques can leave **arc burns** at poor contact points; many aerospace and finished-part specifications prohibit prods entirely.
- **Practical frictions.** Thick or loose coatings mask indications; demagnetization is often required after testing; complex geometries can be hard to field uniformly; and post-cleaning of particle residue is usually expected.

None of these is a flaw of the method - they are its boundaries, and a professional states them plainly when asked why MT was, or was not, the right call.

sections.title: Direction decides detection
sections.body: A discontinuity shows best when magnetic flux crosses it at **45-90 degrees**, and may not show at all when flux runs parallel to it. That single fact drives the most fundamental rule in MT: complete coverage always requires **two magnetizations, roughly perpendicular to each other**. Keep it in mind every time this course mentions circular versus longitudinal fields.

sections.caption: Anatomy of a magnetic particle indication: flux in the part is forced out at a surface discontinuity, forming a leakage field with local poles that holds particles in a visible pattern.

sections.heading: Indication, Discontinuity, Defect
sections.body:
Precise words are half of accurate reporting, and these three are the ones most often abused:

- A **discontinuity** is any interruption in the normal physical structure of a part - a crack, seam, lap, or inclusion. It is a neutral word: a discontinuity may be perfectly acceptable.
- A **defect** is a discontinuity that has been **evaluated and rejected** against the governing acceptance criteria. The word means *failed*. Calling every indication a "defect" in conversation or on paper is the mark of an untrained inspector - and it can have contractual consequences.
- An **indication** is what the test shows you: the particle pattern itself. Indications come in three kinds. A **relevant** indication results from an actual discontinuity. A **nonrelevant** indication has a real magnetic cause that is not a discontinuity - a section change, a drilled hole just under the surface, magnetic writing, or a permeability boundary at a weld. A **false** indication has no magnetic cause at all: particles held by gravity, drainage patterns, or surface roughness.

**Interpretation** is deciding which kind of indication you have. **Evaluation** is judging a relevant indication against the acceptance criteria. The full chain - indication, interpretation, evaluation, disposition - is the skeleton of every report you will ever sign.

sections.caption: Core MT vocabulary used throughout this course.
rows: Magnetic flux
The magnetic field passing through the part, visualized as lines of force
Flux density (B)
Concentration of flux; measured in gauss or tesla (10,000 G = 1 T)
Magnetizing force (H)
The applied force that creates flux; measured in oersteds
Permeability (µ)
How easily a material accepts flux; µ = B/H
Ferromagnetic
Strongly attracted to a magnet and able to concentrate flux (iron, most steels) - the only materials MT can test
Retentivity
Ability to retain a residual field after the magnetizing force is removed; high in hard, high-carbon steels
Coercive force
Reverse magnetizing force required to reduce a residual field to zero
Leakage field
Flux forced out of the part where a discontinuity interrupts it; what holds the particles
Circular magnetization
Flux flowing around the part (e.g., from current through it); reveals longitudinal discontinuities
Longitudinal magnetization
Flux flowing along the part (e.g., from a coil); reveals transverse discontinuities
Continuous method
Particles applied while magnetizing current flows - the standard, most sensitive timing
Residual method
Particles applied after current stops, relying on the retained field; suitable only for high-retentivity parts
Demagnetization
Reducing the residual field to an acceptable level after testing

sections.heading: The Quantities Behind the Words
sections.body:
Four magnetic quantities will follow you through this course, so fix them now.

**Flux density (B)** is how much field is packed through a cross-section of the part, measured in **gauss** or **tesla** - 10,000 gauss to one tesla. **Magnetizing force (H)**, in **oersteds**, is the effort applied to create that flux. The ratio between them, **permeability (µ = B/H)**, is how willingly the material carries flux - the property that makes a low-alloy steel testable and an aluminum fitting untestable. Materials sort into **ferromagnetic** (strongly concentrate flux - iron, nickel, cobalt, most steels), **paramagnetic** (feeble attraction), and **diamagnetic** (feeble repulsion); only the first group supports MT.

When the magnetizing force is removed, some flux remains: the **residual field**, whose strength reflects the material's **retentivity**. Hard, high-carbon and heat-treated steels retain strongly; soft, low-carbon steels do not. The reverse force needed to wipe the residual field to zero is the **coercive force**. These two properties decide more than demagnetization effort: only high-retentivity parts are candidates for the **residual method**, while the **continuous method** works on everything - one reason it is the default in nearly every written procedure.

figures.title: Anatomy of a magnetic particle indication

checkQuestions.question: A longitudinal crack runs along the axis of a steel bar. Which magnetization gives the best chance of detecting it, and why?
options: Longitudinal magnetization, because the flux follows the crack from end to end
Circular magnetization, because its flux runs around the bar and crosses the crack at roughly 90 degrees
Either direction works equally well for any crack
No magnetization can reveal a longitudinal crack
checkQuestions.explanation: Detection requires flux crossing the discontinuity, ideally at 45-90 degrees. Circular flux runs around the bar's circumference, cutting straight across an axial crack; longitudinal flux would run parallel to it and produce little or no leakage.

checkQuestions.question: A particle pattern forms over a real crack, and evaluation shows the crack is within the acceptance limits of the governing specification. The correct description is:
options: A defect, because all cracks are defects
A relevant indication of a discontinuity that is not a defect
A false indication, because the part is acceptable
A nonrelevant indication, because it passed evaluation
checkQuestions.explanation: The indication is relevant (caused by an actual discontinuity), but 'defect' is reserved for discontinuities evaluated as rejectable. An acceptable crack is a discontinuity, not a defect.

checkQuestions.question: Which of these parts is a candidate for magnetic particle testing?
options: An austenitic stainless steel flange
An aluminum pump housing
A low-alloy steel crane hook forging
A titanium compressor blade
checkQuestions.explanation: MT requires a ferromagnetic material. Low-alloy steel is ferromagnetic; austenitic stainless, aluminum, and titanium cannot be usefully magnetized and must be tested by other methods such as PT or ET.

checkQuestions.question: Magnetic permeability is best defined as:
options: The flux remaining in a part after the magnetizing force is removed
The ease with which a material accepts magnetic flux, expressed as B divided by H
The reverse force needed to remove a residual field
The depth to which alternating current penetrates a conductor
checkQuestions.explanation: Permeability µ = B/H relates the flux density achieved to the magnetizing force applied - high permeability means the material concentrates flux easily, the defining trait of ferromagnetic materials.

checkQuestions.question: Particles collect in the rough machining grooves of a part with no magnetic cause involved. This is classified as:
options: A relevant indication
A nonrelevant indication
A false indication
A defect
checkQuestions.explanation: A false indication has no magnetic origin - particles held mechanically by roughness, gravity, or drainage. A nonrelevant indication, by contrast, has a real magnetic cause (like a section change) that is not a discontinuity.

references: CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §1.6 (advantages and limitations)
ASTM E1316 (standard terminology for nondestructive examinations, context)
ASTM E709 (guide) and ASTM E1444 (practice) (method context)
